name: TypeScript Developer
description: Best practices, patterns, and gotchas for TypeScript and Node.js development.

facts.text: Use discriminated unions instead of optional properties for state modeling. Instead of { status?: string; error?: Error; data?: T }, use { status: 'loading' } | { status: 'error'; error: Error } | { status: 'success'; data: T }. TypeScript narrows the type in each branch automatically.

Branded types prevent mixing up primitive values that represent different things. Define: type UserId = string & { readonly __brand: 'UserId' }. Now UserId and OrderId are incompatible even though both are strings. Use a helper function to create them.

Barrel exports (index.ts re-exporting everything) seem clean but cause problems at scale: they break tree-shaking, create circular dependency risks, and slow down TypeScript compilation. Prefer direct imports in large codebases.

Use 'satisfies' operator (TS 4.9+) instead of type annotations when you want type checking without widening. 'const config = { port: 3000 } satisfies Config' keeps the literal type 3000 while still validating against Config.

Never use 'any' — use 'unknown' instead when the type is truly uncertain. 'unknown' forces you to narrow before use, catching bugs at compile time. 'any' silently disables all type checking and spreads through your codebase like a virus.

Use 'as const' for literal types: const ROUTES = { HOME: '/home', ABOUT: '/about' } as const. Without it, TypeScript infers string. With it, TypeScript infers the exact literal values, enabling precise type narrowing.

Zod is the best runtime validation library for TypeScript. Define a schema once, get both runtime validation and TypeScript type with z.infer<typeof schema>. Use it at API boundaries — never trust external data.

Type predicates (x is Type) are powerful but dangerous — TypeScript trusts you completely. A wrong predicate silently breaks type safety. Always pair with runtime checks and test edge cases: function isUser(x: unknown): x is User { return typeof x === 'object' && x !== null && 'id' in x; }

Use 'strict: true' in tsconfig.json from day one. Enabling strict mode later on an existing codebase is painful — hundreds of errors to fix. It enables strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, and other critical safety checks.

Template literal types enable powerful string manipulation at the type level. type EventName = `on${Capitalize<string>}` constrains to strings like 'onClick', 'onHover'. Combined with mapped types, they can auto-generate event handler types from a list of events.

Node.js error handling: always use Error objects, not strings. Wrap external errors: catch (e) { throw new Error('Failed to fetch user', { cause: e }); }. The 'cause' property (ES2022) chains errors without losing the original stack trace.
Use AbortController for cancellation in Node.js. Pass AbortSignal to fetch, setTimeout, and your own async functions. This is the standard pattern — don't invent custom cancellation tokens.

ESM vs CJS in Node.js: use ESM ('type': 'module' in package.json). CJS is legacy. But know the gotchas: __dirname doesn't exist (use import.meta.url), you must include file extensions in imports, and JSON imports need 'with { type: "json" }' or createRequire.

For testing TypeScript, Vitest > Jest. Vitest runs TypeScript natively (no ts-jest config), is faster, has a compatible API, and supports ESM without hacks. Migration from Jest is usually just changing imports.

Test types with expectTypeOf from vitest or tsd. Don't just test runtime behavior — test that your types work: expectTypeOf<ReturnType<typeof getUser>>().toEqualTypeOf<User>(). This catches type regressions.

Use dependency injection over direct imports for testability. Instead of importing a database client at module level, accept it as a parameter: function createUserService(db: Database). This makes mocking trivial without jest.mock hacks.
pnpm > npm > yarn for package management. pnpm uses a content-addressable store (saves disk), enforces strict node_modules (no phantom deps), and is fastest. If you can choose, choose pnpm.

Turborepo or Nx for monorepos. Turborepo is simpler (just caching + task orchestration). Nx is more powerful (code generation, dependency graph analysis). For <10 packages, Turborepo. For large orgs, Nx.

In monorepos, use TypeScript project references for incremental builds. Each package gets its own tsconfig.json with 'composite: true', and the root tsconfig has 'references'. This lets tsc only rebuild changed packages.

The 'infer' keyword in conditional types extracts types from complex structures. type ElementType<T> = T extends Array<infer E> ? E : never; extracts the element type from any array. Master this for writing reusable utility types.

Never use enum — use 'as const' objects or union types instead. Enums generate runtime code, have weird reverse-mapping behavior, and don't work well with type narrowing. const STATUS = { Active: 'active', Inactive: 'inactive' } as const; type Status = typeof STATUS[keyof typeof STATUS];

Use Map<K, V> and Set<T> instead of plain objects for dynamic key-value stores. They have proper iteration order, O(1) size access, work with any key type (not just strings), and don't have prototype pollution risks.

structuredClone() (Node 17+) is the correct way to deep-clone objects. No more JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x)) which loses Dates, undefined, Maps, Sets, and RegExps. structuredClone handles all of these correctly.

Promise.allSettled > Promise.all when you need all results regardless of individual failures. Promise.all short-circuits on first rejection. Promise.allSettled waits for all and returns { status: 'fulfilled', value } | { status: 'rejected', reason } for each.

Use AsyncLocalStorage for request-scoped context (logging, tracing, auth) in Node.js instead of passing context through every function. It's like thread-local storage for async code. Built into Node.js, no dependencies needed.

Type assertion (x as Type) should be a last resort, not a convenience. Each assertion is a point where you're telling TypeScript 'trust me' — and you might be wrong. Prefer type guards, narrowing, and proper typing instead.

Object.keys() returns string[], not (keyof T)[]. This is intentional — TypeScript types are structural, so objects can have extra keys. Use type-safe alternatives: for...in with a narrowing check, or define a typedKeys helper that returns (keyof T)[] when you're certain.

Biome is replacing ESLint + Prettier for many teams. It's a single Rust-based tool that handles linting + formatting, is 10-100x faster, and has sensible defaults. Consider it for new projects — less config, faster CI.
Use tsx (not ts-node) for running TypeScript files directly in development. tsx is faster, handles ESM correctly, and requires zero configuration. Install globally: npm i -g tsx, then: tsx script.ts.

For API response types, define the type and a runtime validator together. Zod schema as source of truth: const UserSchema = z.object({ id: z.string(), name: z.string() }); type User = z.infer<typeof UserSchema>; Then validate at the boundary: UserSchema.parse(apiResponse).

Generic constraints with extends are essential for reusable functions. function getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K] is type-safe. Without the constraint, you'd need type assertions everywhere.

Use the NodeJS.ProcessEnv interface augmentation to type your environment variables: declare global { namespace NodeJS { interface ProcessEnv { DATABASE_URL: string; PORT?: string; } } }. Put this in a env.d.ts file. Now process.env.DATABASE_URL is typed.

Error boundaries in async code: unhandled promise rejections crash Node.js (since v15). Always handle errors in async functions. Use a global process.on('unhandledRejection') as a safety net, but fix the root cause — it means you missed an await or catch somewhere.

Record<string, T> is not the same as { [key: string]: T } in subtle ways, but both allow any string key. If you want a fixed set of keys, use Record<'a' | 'b', T> or a mapped type. For truly dynamic objects, consider Map<string, T> instead.

Use --isolatedModules in tsconfig.json if you use a bundler (Vite, esbuild, swc). These tools transpile files individually and can't handle certain TypeScript features like const enums or namespace merging across files.
